posts.name: Henry Kiichli
posts.cooked:
I was going to write

“How many different Burg producers are in your cellar?”, but that reads like Prince Albert in a can.


As to the title question:

71

posts.name: David Bueker
posts.cooked: 45, though really only about 25, as a bunch are represented by 1 or 2 bottles.

posts.name: Peter Hirsch
posts.cooked:
85 different producers.

If I restrict to 1 case (12 bottles) or more, drops to 64 producers

Also 11 producers represent just over 50% of my Burgundy bottles (51.7%)
So the other 74 producers represent 48.3% of my Burgundy bottles

Top 11 producers, so 50+%:
Roumier
Rousseau
Perrot-Minot
Jadot
de Vogue
Boillot
Fourrier
Arnoux
Clavelier
Leroy
Groffier

To get to the top 66.6% of producers I’d have to expand to 18 producers and add
Cathiard
Barthod
Dujac
Mugnier
Le Moine
Pousse
Bachelet

So that would leave 67 producers for the other 33.3%

I was surprised so many different producers, about 30 more than I would have guessed before looking.

I was surprised by the number of orphans - 22.

fred

posts.name: Sarah Kirschbaum
posts.cooked: Exactly 100 including Beaujolais, which I don’t have time to sort out right now. Like others, many are represented by just a few bottles, but many of those are rare or too expensive to have bought in quantity. Taking that into consideration, I don’t think it’s too many.

posts.name: Dennis Kanagie
posts.cooked:
Wow, there are way less than expected. There are currently only 13 producers represented, but many have multiple vintages / vineyards. I guess we drank up most of the singles while we were searching for the producers and specific bottles we wanted to concentrate on.

Is that reason to or ?

posts.name: Alan Eden
posts.cooked: Zero

posts.name: Sarah Kirschbaum
posts.cooked:
Sarah Kirschbaum:

Exactly 100 including Beaujolais, which I don’t have time to sort out right now. Like others, many are represented by just a few bottles, but many of those are rare or too expensive to have bought in quantity. Taking that into consideration, I don’t think it’s too many.

In fairness, I should add that “my” cellar is really the combination two cellar, mine and Jonathan’s, built separately over many years, which has resulted in a much larger number of producers than my single cellar had. I am pretty sure that, even if both halves of a couple are equally interested in wine, if the two build their collection together, it’s likely they would end up with fewer producers than if two wine collectors build separate collections, which they then combine. Can’t say for sure, but it seems logical to me.

posts.name: Dennis Kanagie
posts.cooked:

In fairness, I should add that “my” cellar is really the combination two cellar, mine and Jonathan’s, built separately over many years, which has resulted in a much larger number of producers than my single cellar had. I am pretty sure that, even if both halves of a couple are equally interested in wine, if the two build their collection together, it’s likely they would end up with fewer producers than if two wine collectors build separate collections, which they then combine. Can’t say for sure, but it seems logical to me.

Which begs the question: did you find new favorite producers / bottles because of Jonathan’s collection or vice versa?

posts.name: Sarah Kirschbaum
posts.cooked:
Dennis Kanagie:
Sarah Kirschbaum:

In fairness, I should add that “my” cellar is really the combination two cellar, mine and Jonathan’s, built separately over many years, which has resulted in a much larger number of producers than my single cellar had. I am pretty sure that, even if both halves of a couple are equally interested in wine, if the two build their collection together, it’s likely they would end up with fewer producers than if two wine collectors build separate collections, which they then combine. Can’t say for sure, but it seems logical to me.

Which begs the question: did you find new favorite producers / bottles because of Jonathan’s collection or vice versa?

Certainly, though more in Riesling than in Burgundy, where he’d focused more on great dry producers than I had, which are now a passion for both of us. I don’t think there were very many Burgundy producers that one of us didn’t know at all before combining cellars. He wasn’t familiar with Clavelier, and there were a few smaller producers I hadn’t encountered, but none that have become areas of focus or favorites. There were, however, a number that we both knew and liked, but only one of us had cellared: Rion, Truchot, Neillon, and Mugneret-Gibourg to name a few.

posts.name: Joshua Kates
posts.cooked: Approx 80- 85, which is notably ridiculous in my case, since i suspect I own quite a few less bottles of Red Burgundy over all than Don, Howard, or Sarah. In fact, I believe there are only 4 Domaines of which i own 12 or more bottles in any vintage from any climat: Pavelot, Bachelet, Jadot, and Fourrier (though if you aggregate the “Mugneret’s,” even leaving out Mongeard-Mugneret, they would make it). The different sites, the different years, the different plays (GC, 1er, village vs. price in any given year) just differ so much, that it’s hardfor me to stick a small group of producers and within them specific wines from specific years (though I am aware generally speaking which wines from which producers tend to be preferred).

posts.name: Stuart BeauneHead Niemtzow
posts.cooked:
1. The plan (in the mid-80s) was to find an array (geographically) of white and red producers accross the Cotes de Nuits and Beaune and Chablis. And, that’s exactly how it panned out…lots of long verticals of a relatively concise group of producers. I always fantasized about having great, intellectually rewarding tastings…and still look for them with my inventory (Cellar Savant, now defunct), but that’s all I do. No demand for them…and my interests have changed.

The whites are much more focused: 6 producers over lots of years in the Cote de Beaune, and 1 producer in Chablis. And, until now I hadn’t realized: that producer in Chablis is by a pretty far margin by largest “representation” in my cellar. And, I’m very glad, even if I’m pretty surprised that this is the case.

Gotta catch up with CellarTracker, this is total guesstimate:

That would be about 4 each Cotes de Nuits and Cotes Chalonnaise, and a dozen Cotes de Beaune.

How do you count Chateau des Jacques Moulin-a-Vent? I know it’s Beaujolais, but it doesn’t taste like it and the ‘producer’ is in Beaune.

Dan Kravitz

I did not count Beaujolais producers. I think of Burgundy and Beaujolais as two different things. As for Chateau des Jacques, I would have included them in Jadot anyway, so I would not have changed my count.
